<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Enumerative combinatorics — counting cleverly</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false,
      macros:{
        '\\Spec':'\\Spec',
        '\\Gal':'\\Gal',
        '\\Hom':'\\Hom',
        '\\tr':'\\tr',
        '\\ad':'\\ad',
        '\\ind':'\\ind'
      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>

1 Binomials & stars-and-bars 2 Inclusion–exclusion 3 Generating functions 4 Permutation statistics 5 Integer partitions 6 Bijective proofs

Enumerative combinatorics

How many things are there? From $\binom{n}{k}$ and stars-and-bars through inclusion–exclusion, generating functions, and permutation statistics, the engine of counting is bijection: turn a hard count into an easy one by mapping onto something you already know.

blocks.html:
1. Binomials, multinomials, and stars-and-bars

The most fundamental count is the binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{k}$ — the number of $k$-element subsets of $\{1,\ldots,n\}$. Two equivalent definitions:

$\displaystyle \binom{n}{k} \;=\; \frac{n!}{k!\,(n-k)!} \;=\; \frac{n(n-1)\cdots(n-k+1)}{k!}.$

The first version is symmetric: $\binom{n}{k}=\binom{n}{n-k}$, mirroring the bijection "subset $\leftrightarrow$ complement". The second is the recipe: arrange $k$ distinguishable choices in $n(n-1)\cdots(n-k+1)$ ways, then quotient by $k!$ to forget the order. The two together yield Pascal's recurrence:

$\displaystyle \binom{n}{k} \;=\; \binom{n-1}{k-1} + \binom{n-1}{k},$

by partitioning $k$-subsets of $[n]$ according to whether they contain the element $n$ or not.

Pascal's triangle
click any cell · highlights its two parents and shows the recursive sum
Click a cell to see Pascal's recurrence in action.

Multinomials

If you want to split $n$ distinguishable objects into ordered bins of sizes $k_1, \ldots, k_r$ (with $k_1+\cdots+k_r = n$), the count is the multinomial coefficient:

$\displaystyle \binom{n}{k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_r} \;=\; \frac{n!}{k_1!\,k_2!\cdots k_r!}.$

It is symmetric in the $k_i$'s. Setting $r=2$ recovers the binomial. The classic application: the number of distinct anagrams of MISSISSIPPI is $\binom{11}{1,4,4,2} = 11!/(1!\,4!\,4!\,2!) = 34650$.

Stars-and-bars

How many ways are there to write $n$ as an ordered sum $x_1 + \cdots + x_r$ with each $x_i \ge 0$? The bijection is the stars-and-bars trick: line up $n$ stars, then drop $r-1$ bars among them to mark the boundaries between bins. Each bin can be empty, and the bars can sit anywhere among the $n+r-1$ slots:

$\displaystyle \#\{\text{weak compositions of }n\text{ into }r\text{ parts}\} \;=\; \binom{n+r-1}{r-1}.$

Example: $n=4, r=3$ gives $\binom{6}{2}=15$ solutions to $x_1+x_2+x_3=4$. Counts of multisets, monomials of fixed degree in fixed variables, and lattice points in simplices all reduce to stars-and-bars.

See also
  • Sets and functions

Used in

<script>
(function(){
  /* SECTION 1: Pascal's triangle clicker */
  const svg = $('#pascal-svg'), out = $('#pascal-out');
  const N = 9; // rows 0..N-1
  const cellW = 56, cellH = 38;
  const padL = 360, padT = 18;

  function binom(n, k){
    if(k < 0 || k > n) return 0;
    if(k === 0 || k === n) return 1;
    k = Math.min(k, n - k);
    let v = 1;
    for(let i = 0; i < k; i++) v = v * (n - i) / (i + 1);
    return Math.round(v);
  }

  let highlight = null; // {n, k}
  function cellPos(n, k){
    const x = padL + (k - n / 2) * cellW;
    const y = padT + n * cellH;
    return [x, y];
  }

  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    for(let n = 0; n < N; n++){
      for(let k = 0; k <= n; k++){
        const [cx, cy] = cellPos(n, k);
        let fill = "var(--panel2)", stroke = "var(--line)", textColor = "var(--ink)";
        if(highlight){
          const isCell = (highlight.n === n && highlight.k === k);
          const isLeftParent = (highlight.n === n + 1 && highlight.k === k + 1);
          const isRightParent = (highlight.n === n + 1 && highlight.k === k);
          if(isCell){ fill = "var(--yellow)"; textColor = "#111"; stroke = "var(--yellow)"; }
          else if(isLeftParent || isRightParent){ fill = "var(--cyan)"; textColor = "#111"; stroke = "var(--cyan)"; }
        }
        const rect = SVG("rect", {
          x: cx - cellW/2 + 2, y: cy - cellH/2 + 2,
          width: cellW - 4, height: cellH - 4, rx: 4,
          fill, stroke, "stroke-width": 1.2,
          "data-n": n, "data-k": k, style: "cursor:pointer"
        });
        svg.appendChild(rect);
        const t = SVG("text", {
          x: cx, y: cy + 4, "text-anchor": "middle",
          "font-size": 13, "font-weight": 600, fill: textColor,
          "pointer-events": "none"
        });
        t.textContent = String(binom(n, k));
        svg.appendChild(t);
      }
    }
  }

  svg.addEventListener("click", (ev) => {
    const t = ev.target;
    if(t.tagName !== "rect") return;
    const n = +t.getAttribute("data-n"), k = +t.getAttribute("data-k");
    highlight = {n, k};
    const v = binom(n, k);
    if(n === 0){
      out.textContent = `C(0,0) = 1 — the empty subset of the empty set.`;
    } else if(k === 0 || k === n){
      out.textContent = `C(${n},${k}) = ${v} — boundary cell (the unique ${k===0 ? "empty" : "full"} subset).`;
    } else {
      const a = binom(n-1, k-1), b = binom(n-1, k);
      out.textContent =
        `C(${n},${k}) = C(${n-1},${k-1}) + C(${n-1},${k}) = ${a} + ${b} = ${v}.\n` +
        `Bijection: ${k}-subsets of [${n}] split by whether element ${n} is in (${a}) or out (${b}).`;
    }
    render();
  });

  render();
})();
</script>
</section>

2. Inclusion–exclusion and derangements

How big is a union? For two sets, $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B|$. The general formula corrects for over-counting at every level of intersection:

$\displaystyle \left| \bigcup_{i=1}^n A_i \right| \;=\; \sum_{\emptyset \ne S \subseteq [n]} (-1)^{|S|+1} \left| \bigcap_{i \in S} A_i \right|.$

Equivalently — and often more useful — the count of elements in none of the $A_i$ is

$\displaystyle \left| \overline{A_1} \cap \cdots \cap \overline{A_n} \right| \;=\; \sum_{S \subseteq [n]} (-1)^{|S|} \left| \bigcap_{i \in S} A_i \right|.$

The proof is one line: each element $x$ in the universe contributes $(1 - 1)^{|T(x)|} = 0$ if it lies in any $A_i$ (where $T(x) = \{i : x \in A_i\}$) and $1$ if it lies in none.

The derangement count

A derangement of $[n]$ is a permutation $\sigma$ with $\sigma(i) \ne i$ for every $i$. Let $A_i$ be the set of permutations fixing $i$. Then $|A_i| = (n-1)!$, $|A_i \cap A_j| = (n-2)!$, and in general $|\bigcap_{i \in S} A_i| = (n-|S|)!$. Inclusion–exclusion:

$\displaystyle D_n \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^n (-1)^k \binom{n}{k} (n-k)! \;=\; n!\sum_{k=0}^n \frac{(-1)^k}{k!}.$

So $D_n / n! \to e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$: the probability that a uniformly random permutation has no fixed points stabilises to $1/e$. The first few values are $D_1=0, D_2=1, D_3=2, D_4=9, D_5=44, D_6=265, \ldots$.

3. Recurrences and generating functions

A sequence $a_0, a_1, a_2, \ldots$ becomes a single object once you encode it as a power series. Two encodings are standard:

$\displaystyle A(x) \;=\; \sum_{n\ge 0} a_n x^n \quad (\text{ordinary}), \qquad \hat A(x) \;=\; \sum_{n\ge 0} a_n \frac{x^n}{n!} \quad (\text{exponential}).$

Operations on sequences become operations on series: shifting, convolution, scaling. A linear recurrence becomes a rational function; a convolution recurrence becomes a polynomial equation in $A(x)$.

The Catalan numbers

The Catalan numbers $C_0, C_1, C_2, \ldots = 1, 1, 2, 5, 14, 42, \ldots$ count many things: Dyck paths, triangulations, binary trees, non-crossing partitions. The fundamental recurrence is

$\displaystyle C_{n+1} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} C_k\,C_{n-k}.$

(A non-empty Dyck path of $2(n+1)$ steps starts with an up-step, returns to the axis at some point, splitting into a Dyck path of length $2k$ and one of length $2(n-k)$.) Multiplying by $x^{n+1}$ and summing,

$\displaystyle C(x) - 1 \;=\; x\,C(x)^2.$

This is a quadratic in $C(x)$, with the branch chosen so $C(0)=1$:

$\displaystyle C(x) \;=\; \frac{1 - \sqrt{1 - 4x}}{2x}.$

Expanding $\sqrt{1 - 4x}$ via the generalised binomial theorem and simplifying yields the closed form:

$\displaystyle C_n \;=\; \frac{1}{n+1}\binom{2n}{n}.$

<script>
(function(){
  /* SECTION 3: generating-function coefficient extractor */
  const sel = $('#gf-series'), nIn = $('#gf-n'), nDisp = $('#gf-ndisp');
  const svg = $('#gf-svg'), out = $('#gf-out');

  function fact(k){ let v=1; for(let i=2;i<=k;i++) v *= i; return v; }
  function binom(n, k){
    if(k < 0 || k > n) return 0;
    let v = 1;
    for(let i = 0; i < k; i++) v = v * (n - i) / (i + 1);
    return Math.round(v);
  }
  function fib(n){ if(n<=0) return 0; let a=1, b=1; for(let i=2;i<=n;i++){ const c=a+b; a=b; b=c; } return n===1?1:b; }
  function catalan(n){ return Math.round(binom(2*n, n) / (n + 1)); }

  function coef(name, n){
    if(name === "geom") return 1;
    if(name === "exp"){
      // a_n = 1, since e^x = sum x^n / n! and the coefficient of x^n / n! is 1; we display a_n = 1
      return 1;
    }
    if(name === "catalan") return catalan(n);
    if(name === "fib") return fib(n);
    if(name === "binom4") return n <= 4 ? binom(4, n) : 0;
    return 0;
  }
  function label(name){
    return ({
      geom: "[x^n] 1/(1-x) = 1 (constant sequence)",
      exp: "[x^n/n!] e^x = 1 (so [x^n] = 1/n!)",
      catalan: "[x^n] C(x) = C_n = (1/(n+1)) * C(2n, n)",
      fib: "[x^n] x/(1-x-x^2) = F_n (Fibonacci, F_1=F_2=1)",
      binom4: "[x^n] (1+x)^4 = C(4,n)"
    })[name] || "";
  }

  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const name = sel.value;
    const n = +nIn.value;
    nDisp.textContent = n;
    const maxN = 12;
    const vals = [];
    let maxV = 1;
    for(let i = 0; i <= maxN; i++){
      const v = coef(name, i);
      vals.push(v);
      if(v > maxV) maxV = v;
    }
    // bar chart
    const W = 720, H = 220, padX = 60, padY = 30;
    const bw = (W - 2*padX) / (maxN + 1);
    // axis
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line", {x1:padX, y1:H-padY, x2:W-padX, y2:H-padY, stroke:"var(--line)", "stroke-width":1}));
    for(let i = 0; i <= maxN; i++){
      const x = padX + i*bw + 4;
      const v = vals[i];
      const h = v > 0 ? (H - 2*padY) * v / maxV : 0;
      const y = H - padY - h;
      const isActive = (i === n);
      svg.appendChild(SVG("rect", {
        x, y, width: bw - 8, height: h,
        fill: isActive ? "var(--yellow)" : "var(--cyan)",
        "fill-opacity": isActive ? 0.95 : 0.35,
        stroke: isActive ? "var(--yellow)" : "var(--cyan)",
        "stroke-width": 1
      }));
      const t = SVG("text", {
        x: x + (bw-8)/2, y: H - padY + 14,
        "text-anchor": "middle", "font-size": 11,
        fill: isActive ? "var(--yellow)" : "var(--mute)"
      });
      t.textContent = String(i);
      svg.appendChild(t);
      if(isActive){
        const tv = SVG("text", {
          x: x + (bw-8)/2, y: y - 6,
          "text-anchor": "middle", "font-size": 11,
          fill: "var(--yellow)", "font-weight": 700
        });
        tv.textContent = String(v);
        svg.appendChild(tv);
      }
    }
    out.textContent =
      `${label(name)}\n` +
      `a_${n} = ${vals[n]}` +
      (name === "exp" ? `   (so 1/${fact(n)} = ${(1/fact(n)).toExponential(3)})` : "");
  }
  sel.addEventListener("change", render);
  nIn.addEventListener("input", render);
  render();
})();
</script>
</section>

4. Permutation statistics

$|S_n| = n!$ is the trivial count. The interesting questions are refined: how many permutations have $k$ inversions, $k$ descents, $k$ cycles? Each statistic gives a polynomial whose specialisation at $x=1$ is $n!$.

Inversions

An inversion of $\sigma \in S_n$ is a pair $i < j$ with $\sigma(i) > \sigma(j)$. The total inversion count $\mathrm{inv}(\sigma)$ measures how far $\sigma$ is from the identity. The generating polynomial

$\displaystyle \sum_{\sigma \in S_n} q^{\mathrm{inv}(\sigma)} \;=\; [n]_q! \;=\; \prod_{k=1}^n \frac{1 - q^k}{1 - q}$

is the $q$-factorial. At $q=1$ it specialises to $n!$; at $q=0$ it gives $1$ (only the identity has zero inversions).

Descents and Eulerian numbers

A descent of $\sigma$ is a position $i$ with $\sigma(i) > \sigma(i+1)$. The Eulerian number $\langle {n\atop k}\rangle$ counts permutations with exactly $k$ descents. Worpitzky's identity packages them: $x^n = \sum_k \langle {n\atop k}\rangle \binom{x+k}{n}$.

Cycles and Stirling numbers

The (signless) Stirling number of the first kind $c(n, k)$ counts permutations of $[n]$ with exactly $k$ cycles. Their generating identity is the rising factorial:

$\displaystyle x(x+1)(x+2)\cdots(x+n-1) \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} c(n,k)\,x^k.$

At $x=1$ this is $n!$, recovering $\sum_k c(n,k) = n!$. The cycle-index polynomial of $S_n$ encodes the full cycle-type distribution and is the engine behind Pólya enumeration of colourings under group actions.

<script>
(function(){
  /* SECTION 4: permutation-statistics calculator */
  const inp = $('#perm-input'), btnRand = $('#perm-rand'), btnId = $('#perm-id');
  const svg = $('#perm-svg'), out = $('#perm-out');

  function parsePerm(s){
    const toks = s.trim().split(/[\s,]+/).filter(Boolean).map(Number);
    if(toks.some(x => !Number.isFinite(x) || x < 1 || !Number.isInteger(x))) return null;
    const seen = new Set(toks);
    if(seen.size !== toks.length) return null;
    const n = toks.length;
    for(let i = 1; i <= n; i++) if(!seen.has(i)) return null;
    return toks;
  }
  function inversions(p){
    let c = 0;
    for(let i = 0; i < p.length; i++) for(let j = i+1; j < p.length; j++) if(p[i] > p[j]) c++;
    return c;
  }
  function descents(p){
    let c = 0;
    for(let i = 0; i < p.length - 1; i++) if(p[i] > p[i+1]) c++;
    return c;
  }
  function cycles(p){
    const n = p.length, seen = Array(n).fill(false);
    const cs = [];
    for(let i = 0; i < n; i++){
      if(seen[i]) continue;
      const cyc = [];
      let j = i;
      while(!seen[j]){ seen[j] = true; cyc.push(j+1); j = p[j] - 1; }
      cs.push(cyc);
    }
    return cs;
  }

  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const p = parsePerm(inp.value);
    if(!p){
      out.textContent = "Not a valid permutation of [1..n]: enter each integer 1..n exactly once.";
      return;
    }
    const n = p.length;
    const W = 720, H = 200, padX = 60, padY = 30;
    const dx = (W - 2*padX) / Math.max(1, n - 1);
    const yTop = padY, yBot = H - padY;
    // crossing diagram: top row = i, bottom row = sigma(i)
    for(let i = 0; i < n; i++){
      const xTop = padX + i*dx;
      const xBot = padX + (p[i] - 1)*dx;
      svg.appendChild(SVG("line", {
        x1: xTop, y1: yTop, x2: xBot, y2: yBot,
        stroke: "var(--cyan)", "stroke-width": 1.4, "stroke-opacity": 0.7
      }));
    }
    // top labels
    for(let i = 0; i < n; i++){
      const x = padX + i*dx;
      const t = SVG("text", {x, y: yTop - 8, "text-anchor":"middle", "font-size":12, fill:"var(--mute)"});
      t.textContent = String(i+1);
      svg.appendChild(t);
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle", {cx:x, cy:yTop, r:3, fill:"var(--ink)"}));
    }
    // bottom labels
    for(let j = 0; j < n; j++){
      const x = padX + j*dx;
      const t = SVG("text", {x, y: yBot + 18, "text-anchor":"middle", "font-size":12, fill:"var(--yellow)"});
      t.textContent = String(j+1);
      svg.appendChild(t);
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle", {cx:x, cy:yBot, r:3, fill:"var(--yellow)"}));
    }
    const inv = inversions(p), des = descents(p), cs = cycles(p);
    const cyc = cs.map(c => "(" + c.join(" ") + ")").join(" ");
    out.textContent =
      `n = ${n}\n` +
      `one-line: ${p.join(" ")}\n` +
      `inversions = ${inv}    descents = ${des}    cycles = ${cs.length}: ${cyc}`;
  }

  inp.addEventListener("input", render);
  btnRand.addEventListener("click", () => {
    const n = 6 + Math.floor(Math.random()*4);
    const arr = [];
    for(let i = 1; i <= n; i++) arr.push(i);
    for(let i = arr.length - 1; i > 0; i--){
      const j = Math.floor(Math.random()*(i+1));
      [arr[i], arr[j]] = [arr[j], arr[i]];
    }
    inp.value = arr.join(" ");
    render();
  });
  btnId.addEventListener("click", () => {
    inp.value = "1 2 3 4 5 6";
    render();
  });
  render();
})();
</script>
</section>

5. Integer partitions and Euler's product

A partition of $n$ is a way to write $n = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \cdots + \lambda_r$ with $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \cdots \ge \lambda_r \ge 1$. The function $p(n)$ counts these. The first few values:

$\displaystyle p(0) = 1,\; p(1) = 1,\; p(2) = 2,\; p(3) = 3,\; p(4) = 5,\; p(5) = 7,\; p(6) = 11,\; p(7) = 15,\; \ldots$

There is no closed form, but Hardy–Ramanujan's asymptotic formula $p(n) \sim \frac{1}{4n\sqrt{3}}\,e^{\pi\sqrt{2n/3}}$ is one of the gems of analytic combinatorics. The starting point is Euler's infinite product:

$\displaystyle \sum_{n\ge 0} p(n)\,q^n \;=\; \prod_{k\ge 1} \frac{1}{1 - q^k}.$

The factor $1/(1-q^k) = 1 + q^k + q^{2k} + \cdots$ chooses how many parts of size $k$ to use; multiplying across all $k$ enumerates partitions exactly.

Young diagrams

A partition $\lambda$ is drawn as a left-justified array of boxes, the Young diagram. Conjugation — reflecting across the main diagonal — gives a bijection $\lambda \mapsto \lambda'$ between partitions of $n$ that preserves the count but swaps "number of parts" with "largest part".

Young-diagram drawer
click to add or remove a box · the partition stays left- and top-justified
6

Rogers–Ramanujan

The deepest classical partition identities are due to Rogers and Ramanujan. They assert the surprising equinumeration:

Rogers–Ramanujan (first identity). For each $n$, the partitions of $n$ in which any two parts differ by at least $2$ equal the partitions of $n$ into parts congruent to $\pm 1 \pmod 5$.

Equivalently, as power-series identities,

$\displaystyle \sum_{n\ge 0} \frac{q^{n^2}}{(1-q)(1-q^2)\cdots(1-q^n)} \;=\; \prod_{n\ge 0}\frac{1}{(1-q^{5n+1})(1-q^{5n+4})}.$

Both sides equal $\sum_{n} a_n q^n$ where $a_n$ is the common count. There is no easy combinatorial bijection between the two families — the original proofs are analytic, and a bijective proof (Garsia–Milne) is genuinely intricate.

Used in
Enumerative combinatorics · Bijective proofs

<script>
(function(){
  /* SECTION 5: Young-diagram drawer */
  const nIn = $('#young-n'), nDisp = $('#young-ndisp');
  const svg = $('#young-svg'), out = $('#young-out');
  const btnConj = $('#young-conj'), btnSt = $('#young-staircase'), btnRect = $('#young-rect');

  let lambda = [3, 2, 1]; // current partition

  function setN(n){ nIn.value = String(n); nDisp.textContent = n; }
  function totalSize(l){ return l.reduce((a,b)=>a+b, 0); }
  function fitToN(l, n){
    // truncate to total size <= n, padding with single boxes where possible
    let s = totalSize(l);
    while(s > n){
      // shrink last column or last part
      const i = l.length - 1;
      if(l[i] > 1) l[i] -= 1; else l.pop();
      s = totalSize(l);
    }
    while(s < n){
      // grow: add a box where it preserves weak-decreasing order
      if(l.length === 0 || l[l.length-1] === 1) l.push(1);
      else if(l[0] === Infinity) break;
      else { l[l.length-1] += 1; }
      s = totalSize(l);
    }
    return l;
  }
  function conjugate(l){
    if(l.length === 0) return [];
    const m = l[0];
    const c = [];
    for(let i = 1; i <= m; i++){
      let r = 0;
      for(const part of l) if(part >= i) r++;
      c.push(r);
    }
    return c;
  }
  function staircase(n){
    // 4+3+2+1 = 10 baseline; otherwise greedy staircase for n
    const l = [];
    let k = 1, s = 0;
    while(true){
      const cand = [];
      let total = 0;
      for(let i = k; i >= 1; i--){ cand.push(i); total += i; }
      if(total <= n){ l.length = 0; l.push(...cand); k++; }
      else break;
    }
    return fitToN(l.length ? l : [1], n);
  }
  function rectangle(n){
    if(n === 1) return [1];
    if(n === 2) return [2];
    if(n === 3) return [3];
    if(n === 4) return [2,2];
    if(n === 5) return [3,2];
    if(n === 6) return [3,3];
    if(n === 7) return [4,3];
    if(n === 8) return [4,4];
    if(n === 9) return [3,3,3];
    if(n === 10) return [5,5];
    return [n];
  }

  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const n = +nIn.value;
    nDisp.textContent = n;
    if(totalSize(lambda) !== n){ lambda = fitToN(lambda.slice(), n); }
    const W = 720, H = 320, cell = 36, padL = 60, padT = 30;
    const colours = ["var(--yellow)", "var(--cyan)", "var(--pink)", "var(--violet)", "var(--green)"];
    lambda.forEach((part, i) => {
      const colour = colours[i % colours.length];
      for(let j = 0; j < part; j++){
        const x = padL + j * cell, y = padT + i * cell;
        svg.appendChild(SVG("rect", {
          x, y, width: cell - 2, height: cell - 2, rx: 3,
          fill: colour, "fill-opacity": 0.25, stroke: colour, "stroke-width": 1.2
        }));
      }
    });
    // axis hints
    const ax = SVG("text", {x: padL, y: padT - 8, "font-size": 11, fill: "var(--mute)"});
    ax.textContent = `λ = (${lambda.join(", ")})`;
    svg.appendChild(ax);
    out.textContent =
      `n = ${n},  λ = (${lambda.join(", ")}),  parts = ${lambda.length},  largest = ${lambda[0] || 0}\n` +
      `conjugate λ' = (${conjugate(lambda).join(", ")})`;
  }

  nIn.addEventListener("input", () => {
    const n = +nIn.value;
    lambda = fitToN(lambda.slice(), n);
    render();
  });
  btnConj.addEventListener("click", () => { lambda = conjugate(lambda); render(); });
  btnSt.addEventListener("click", () => { lambda = staircase(+nIn.value); render(); });
  btnRect.addEventListener("click", () => { lambda = rectangle(+nIn.value); render(); });
  render();
})();
</script>

6. Bijective proofs

Worked example: Catalan bijection.

The most satisfying proof of $|A| = |B|$ is a bijection $\varphi : A \to B$ — an explicit invertible map. Such a proof gives more than the count: it transports structure from one side to the other. The Catalan numbers are the prime example, with at least 200 known Catalan-counted families and a forest of bijections among them.

Dyck paths and triangulations

A Dyck path of length $2n$ is a lattice path from $(0,0)$ to $(2n,0)$ using steps $(+1,+1)$ (up) and $(+1,-1)$ (down) that never dips below the $x$-axis. There are $C_n$ of them.

A triangulation of a convex $(n+2)$-gon is a maximal set of non-crossing diagonals; equivalently, a way to cut the polygon into $n$ triangles. There are also $C_n$ of these.

The bijection: fix a base edge of the polygon. Walk the triangulation tree rooted at that edge in pre-order, recording an "up" step whenever you enter a child triangle and a "down" step whenever you back out. The resulting word of length $2n$ is a Dyck path.

Catalan path ↔ triangulation
step through the $C_n$ pairs · watch the bijection synchronise
0

RSK and Young tableaux

The Robinson–Schensted–Knuth correspondence is a bijection between non-negative integer matrices and pairs $(P, Q)$ of semistandard Young tableaux of the same shape. Restricted to permutation matrices it bijects $S_n$ with pairs of standard tableaux of the same shape, yielding the spectacular identity

$\displaystyle n! \;=\; \sum_{\lambda \vdash n} f^\lambda \cdot f^\lambda \;=\; \sum_{\lambda \vdash n} (f^\lambda)^2,$

where $f^\lambda$ is the number of standard Young tableaux of shape $\lambda$. The tableau pair carries an enormous amount of structure: the shape determines the longest increasing subsequence (Schensted's theorem), and RSK underlies the entire combinatorics of symmetric functions, Schur polynomials, and representations of $S_n$.

<script>
(function(){
  /* SECTION 6: Catalan path <-> triangulation bijection */
  const nSel = $('#bij-n'), iIn = $('#bij-i'), iDisp = $('#bij-idx');
  const btnPrev = $('#bij-prev'), btnNext = $('#bij-next');
  const svg = $('#bij-svg'), out = $('#bij-out');

  // generate all Dyck words of length 2n in lex order
  function dyckWords(n){
    const out = [];
    function rec(w, ups, downs){
      if(w.length === 2*n){ out.push(w); return; }
      if(ups < n) rec(w + "U", ups + 1, downs);
      if(downs < ups) rec(w + "D", ups, downs + 1);
    }
    rec("", 0, 0);
    return out;
  }

  // For a Dyck word w of length 2n, build a binary tree (left = first child of triangulation under base edge, right = sibling).
  // Then convert to a triangulation of an (n+2)-gon by recording the recursive splits.
  // We use the standard recursive bijection: a Dyck word w = U w1 D w2 corresponds to a triangulation built from two sub-triangulations.
  // For drawing, we compute, for each non-empty Dyck word, the "split index" k such that the apex triangle of the polygon has vertex k.
  function dyckToTriangulation(w, baseStart, baseEnd){
    // returns list of triangles as triples [i,j,k] (vertex indices around the polygon)
    if(w.length === 0) return [];
    // split: U w1 D w2  -> w1 is balanced
    let bal = 0;
    let split = -1;
    for(let i = 1; i < w.length; i++){
      if(w[i-1] === "U") bal++; else bal--;
      // first time we return to depth 0 corresponds to closing the initial U
      // but our word starts with U at index 0; depth after position 0 is 1.
      // After the matching D, depth returns to 0.
      if(bal === 0){ split = i; break; }
    }
    if(split === -1) return [];
    const w1 = w.slice(1, split);
    const w2 = w.slice(split + 1);
    const n1 = w1.length / 2;
    // The apex of the polygon over baseStart..baseEnd is at vertex baseStart + 1 + n1
    const apex = baseStart + 1 + n1;
    const tri = [[baseStart, apex, baseEnd]];
    return tri.concat(
      dyckToTriangulation(w1, baseStart, apex),
      dyckToTriangulation(w2, apex, baseEnd)
    );
  }

  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const n = +nSel.value;
    const words = dyckWords(n);
    iIn.max = String(words.length - 1);
    let idx = +iIn.value;
    if(idx >= words.length) { idx = words.length - 1; iIn.value = String(idx); }
    iDisp.textContent = String(idx);
    const w = words[idx];

    // left half: Dyck path
    const padL = 50, padT = 50, cell = 30;
    const baseY = padT + n * cell;
    // axis
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line", {
      x1: padL, y1: baseY, x2: padL + 2*n*cell, y2: baseY,
      stroke: "var(--line)", "stroke-width": 1.2
    }));
    let x = padL, y = baseY;
    let path = `M${x} ${y}`;
    for(const c of w){
      const dy = (c === "U") ? -cell : cell;
      x += cell; y += dy;
      path += ` L${x} ${y}`;
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG("path", {
      d: path, fill: "none", stroke: "var(--cyan)", "stroke-width": 2
    }));
    // markers
    let cx = padL, cy = baseY;
    svg.appendChild(SVG("circle", {cx, cy, r: 3, fill: "var(--cyan)"}));
    for(const c of w){
      cx += cell; cy += (c === "U") ? -cell : cell;
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle", {cx, cy, r: 3, fill: "var(--cyan)"}));
    }
    // word label
    const wt = SVG("text", {
      x: padL, y: padT - 18, "font-size": 12, fill: "var(--yellow)",
      "font-family": "ui-monospace,monospace"
    });
    wt.textContent = "Dyck word: " + w;
    svg.appendChild(wt);

    // right half: polygon + triangulation
    const N = n + 2;
    const polyCx = 540, polyCy = 160, R = 110;
    const verts = [];
    // place vertex 0 at the bottom-right and 1 at the bottom-left so the "base edge" 0..1 is at the bottom
    for(let k = 0; k < N; k++){
      const angle = -Math.PI/2 + 2*Math.PI*k/N + Math.PI; // start near bottom
      verts.push([polyCx + R*Math.cos(angle), polyCy + R*Math.sin(angle)]);
    }
    // polygon edges
    for(let k = 0; k < N; k++){
      const a = verts[k], b = verts[(k+1) % N];
      svg.appendChild(SVG("line", {
        x1:a[0], y1:a[1], x2:b[0], y2:b[1],
        stroke: "var(--violet)", "stroke-width": 1.5
      }));
    }
    // diagonals from triangulation
    const triangles = dyckToTriangulation(w, 0, N - 1);
    const diagSet = new Set();
    for(const [i,j,k] of triangles){
      for(const [a,b] of [[i,j],[j,k],[i,k]]){
        const lo = Math.min(a,b), hi = Math.max(a,b);
        if(hi - lo > 1 && !(lo === 0 && hi === N - 1)) diagSet.add(`${lo}-${hi}`);
      }
    }
    for(const key of diagSet){
      const [lo, hi] = key.split("-").map(Number);
      const a = verts[lo], b = verts[hi];
      svg.appendChild(SVG("line", {
        x1:a[0], y1:a[1], x2:b[0], y2:b[1],
        stroke: "var(--yellow)", "stroke-width": 1.6, "stroke-opacity": 0.85
      }));
    }
    // vertex markers
    verts.forEach((v, k) => {
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle", {cx:v[0], cy:v[1], r: 4, fill: "var(--violet)"}));
      const t = SVG("text", {
        x: polyCx + (v[0]-polyCx)*1.18, y: polyCy + (v[1]-polyCy)*1.18 + 4,
        "text-anchor": "middle", "font-size": 11, fill: "var(--mute)"
      });
      t.textContent = String(k);
      svg.appendChild(t);
    });

    out.textContent =
      `n = ${n}, total Catalan triangulations C_${n} = ${words.length}\n` +
      `index ${idx} of ${words.length - 1}    Dyck word: ${w}\n` +
      `triangulation diagonals: ${diagSet.size === 0 ? "(none — already a triangle)" : [...diagSet].map(s => "(" + s.replace("-", ",") + ")").join(" ")}`;
  }

  nSel.addEventListener("change", () => { iIn.value = "0"; render(); });
  iIn.addEventListener("input", render);
  btnPrev.addEventListener("click", () => {
    const v = +iIn.value;
    if(v > 0){ iIn.value = String(v - 1); render(); }
  });
  btnNext.addEventListener("click", () => {
    const v = +iIn.value, max = +iIn.max;
    if(v < max){ iIn.value = String(v + 1); render(); }
  });
  render();
})();
</script>
</section>

7. Connections

The generating-function / bijective-proof / cycle-index trinity is the workhorse of combinatorial counting — a small toolkit that surfaces almost everywhere a counting question arises.

Algebra and representations

Symmetric functions and the cycle index polynomial connect counting to representation theory; the RSK correspondence and Schur–Weyl duality are the bridge to character theory of $S_n$ and $\mathrm{GL}_n$.

Probability and probabilistic method

Generating-function methods (Wilf's "snake oil", saturation) come up in probability for moment problems; probabilistic existence proofs are often the only way to count an extremal object.

Algebraic combinatorics and posets

The Möbius function on posets generalises inclusion-exclusion; matroid theory, lattice theory, and Stanley's "Combinatorics and Commutative Algebra" all rest on this.

Analytic combinatorics

Singularity analysis of generating functions (Flajolet–Sedgewick) gives asymptotics of combinatorial sequences from the analytic structure of their OGFs/EGFs — the bridge to complex analysis via residues.

Open frontiers: cluster algebras (Fomin–Zelevinsky); flag algebras (Razborov, computer-assisted extremal proofs); positivity questions (Schubert calculus, Macdonald polynomials); algebraic combinatorics via Hopf algebras and quasi-symmetric functions.
